title: Exceptions and guarantees

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Exceptions and guarantees; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A vector update builds a replacement first and swaps only after success, preserving the original state if allocation throws. Your goal is to implement an operation with a stated basic or strong exception-safety guarantee. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and initialization; Value, reference and const-reference parameters; RAII and deterministic cleanup; Class interface and invariants. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Exceptions transfer control to a matching handler while stack unwinding destroys completed automatic objects, enabling RAII cleanup and explicit basic, strong, or no-throw guarantees. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Destructors should not emit exceptions during unwinding, noexcept violations terminate, catch order matters, and exceptions should not replace expected routine branching indiscriminately. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: When an exception occurs, automatic objects in exited scopes are abandoned without destructors running.

includes: Throw meaningful failures, catch at responsible boundaries and preserve invariants.
Implement an operation with a stated basic or strong exception-safety guarantee.
excludes: Destructors should not emit exceptions during unwinding, noexcept violations terminate, catch order matters, and exceptions should not replace expected routine branching indiscriminately.

outcomes: Implement an operation with a stated basic or strong exception-safety guarantee.

examples.title: Exceptions and guarantees: defined case
examples.situation: A vector update builds a replacement first and swaps only after success, preserving the original state if allocation throws.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A vector update builds a replacement first and swaps only after success, preserving the original state if allocation throws.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Stack unwinding destroys fully constructed automatic objects; RAII uses those destructors to release resources on exceptional exits.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Stack unwinding destroys fully constructed automatic objects; RAII uses those destructors to release resources on exceptional exits.

examples.title: Exceptions and guarantees: boundary case
examples.situation: A catch block handles a specific parse_error before a broader std::exception handler and preserves useful context.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A catch block handles a specific parse_error before a broader std::exception handler and preserves useful context.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Destructors should not emit exceptions during unwinding, noexcept violations terminate, catch order matters, and exceptions should not replace expected routine branching indiscriminately.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: When an exception occurs, automatic objects in exited scopes are abandoned without destructors running.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement an operation with a stated basic or strong exception-safety guarantee.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: When an exception occurs, automatic objects in exited scopes are abandoned without destructors running.
misconceptions.correction: Stack unwinding destroys fully constructed automatic objects; RAII uses those destructors to release resources on exceptional exits.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: When an exception occurs, automatic objects in exited scopes are abandoned without destructors running.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Exceptions and guarantees: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Destructors should not emit exceptions during unwinding, noexcept violations terminate, catch order matters, and exceptions should not replace expected routine branching indiscriminately.
